topic: Someone receives an apology letter years after the incident
raw_output:
[story 1]

The envelope was thick, cream-colored, sealed with wax that looked ancient. Elias traced the embossed lettering with a fingertip, his breath catching in his throat. It had been fifteen years since the argument, the sharp words that had fractured everything between him and Clara. He hadn't expected a reply, certainly not one bearing such formal weight. His hands, usually steady when handling tools, were trembling slightly as he slid the letter onto the worn wooden desk. He reread the opening lines, the script elegant and undeniably hers. A strange tightening began in his chest, a flutter that made his stomach clench. He wanted to burn the paper, to destroy the evidence of this unexpected connection, but his fingers remained frozen above the parchment. He swallowed hard, the dryness in his mouth making the simple act of breathing feel monumental.

[story 2]

When the mail slot rattled, Maya pulled the letter out, her fingers brushing against the heavy paper as if it might shatter. The ink was faded, the paper brittle. She hadn't anticipated anything from Liam, not even a scrap of correspondence. A peculiar sensation washed over her—a sudden lightness followed by a profound internal bracing. She unfolded the note slowly, her eyes scanning the familiar handwriting. A shallow intake of air escaped her lips. Her palms began to sweat, dampening the edges of the paper. Every nerve ending seemed hyper-aware, cataloging the silence of the apartment around her. She reread the apology, her gaze lingering on the signature. A tremor ran through her arm, making her grip tighten painfully on the edges of the letter.

[story 3]

Arthur found the letter tucked beneath a stack of old tax documents. The paper felt strangely warm under his touch. He sat on the edge of the sofa, the silence of the late afternoon amplifying the sound of his own pulse drumming in his ears. He hadn't anticipated this intrusion from Sarah. He unfolded the stationery, his gaze darting back and forth across the lines of text. A distinct pressure built behind his eyes, a physical manifestation of apprehension. He cleared his throat, a dry, rasping sound, before forcing himself to read the words. His jaw clenched so tightly he could feel the strain in his molars. When he finally looked up, the room seemed suddenly too large, and he wished he could simply dissolve into the upholstery.
